source_paper_title: A Superlinearly Convergent Subgradient Method for Sharp Semismooth Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Charisopoulos_2024, title={A Superlinearly Convergent Subgradient Method for Sharp Semismooth Problems}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1390}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1390}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Charisopoulos, Vasileios and Davis, Damek}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1678–1709} }
context:
Section 5.2.2 (Max-linear regression and PolyakSGM), page 38

We do not attempt to verify assumptions (A1) and (A2) for this problem class. Instead, we note that if the solution set is isolated, semialgebraicity of \(f\) implies that we (A2) holds (see Proposition 2.1). On the other hand, verifying Assumption (A1) is remains an intriguing problem.
title: Verify sharp growth constant for max-linear regression with absolute loss setting
background_latex:
Let \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{R}^d\) be given measurement vectors and let \(\bar y\in\mathbb{R}^m\) be given observations. For an integer \(r\ge 1\), define parameters \(\beta=(\beta_1,\dots,\beta_r)\) with \(\beta_j\in\mathbb{R}^d\). Define the max-linear predictor
\[
\hat y_i(\beta):=\max_{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}}\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle,\qquad i=1,\dots,m.
\]
Consider the nonsmooth objective
\[
f(\beta):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bigl|\bar y_i-\hat y_i(\beta)\bigr|.
\]
Let \(f^*:=\inf_{\beta} f(\beta)\) and \(X^*:=\operatorname{argmin} f\) denote the optimal value and set of minimizers (assumed nonempty). For \(x\) in a Euclidean space, \(\mathrm{dist}(x,X^*):=\inf_{z\in X^*}\|x-z\|\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine conditions (and/or prove in the intended statistical model) under which there exists \(\mu>0\) and a neighborhood \(U\) of a minimizer \(\bar\beta\in X^*\) such that the sharp growth (error bound) inequality
\[
f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\qquad\text{for all }\beta\in U
\]
holds for the max-linear regression objective \(f\) defined above.
latex:
Let \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{R}^d\) be given measurement vectors and let \(\bar y\in\mathbb{R}^m\) be given observations. For an integer \(r\ge 1\), define parameters \(\beta=(\beta_1,\dots,\beta_r)\) with \(\beta_j\in\mathbb{R}^d\). Define the max-linear predictor
\[
\hat y_i(\beta):=\max_{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}}\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle,\qquad i=1,\dots,m.
\]
Consider the nonsmooth objective
\[
f(\beta):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bigl|\bar y_i-\hat y_i(\beta)\bigr|.
\]
Let \(f^*:=\inf_{\beta} f(\beta)\) and \(X^*:=\operatorname{argmin} f\) denote the optimal value and set of minimizers (assumed nonempty). For \(x\) in a Euclidean space, \(\mathrm{dist}(x,X^*):=\inf_{z\in X^*}\|x-z\|\).

Determine conditions (and/or prove in the intended statistical model) under which there exists \(\mu>0\) and a neighborhood \(U\) of a minimizer \(\bar\beta\in X^*\) such that the sharp growth (error bound) inequality
\[
f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\qquad\text{for all }\beta\in U
\]
holds for the max-linear regression objective \(f\) defined above.
open_problem_quote: We do not attempt to verify assumptions (A1) and (A2) for this problem class. Instead, we note that if the solution set is isolated, semialgebraicity of \(f\) implies that we (A2) holds (see Proposition 2.1). On the other hand, verifying Assumption (A1) is remains an intriguing problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Sharpness can be empirically tested on finite-dimensional instances by approximating minimizers and checking local ratios \((f(\beta)-f^*)/\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\), but computing \(\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) and certifying \(f^*\) and \(X^*\) is itself nontrivial. Automated searches might still find instances where sharpness fails or appears to hold with small \(\mu\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could generate random designs \(a_i\) and ground-truth parameters, numerically solve for \(X^*\), and optimize over perturbations to estimate lower bounds on the sharpness modulus \(\mu\) or find apparent violations. The fitness signal (estimated minimal local ratio) is computable but noisy and sensitive to numerical optimization accuracy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem from Charisopoulos--Davis asks for verifiable conditions ensuring local sharp growth \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for max-linear regression with absolute loss. In the source paper, sharp growth (Assumption (A1)) is the key regularity enabling linear convergence of Polyak-type subgradient steps and, when combined with (b)-regularity/semismoothness (Assumption (A2)), yields the superlinear improvement mechanism of PolyakBundle and the SuperPolyak scheme. The max-linear absolute-loss objective is semialgebraic and piecewise linear (hence semismooth/(b)-regular is plausible), but the missing ingredient is precisely (A1): a data/model-dependent error bound constant \(\mu\) near \(X^*\).

Forward-citing work largely develops algorithmic and variational-analytic frameworks that take growth/error bounds as assumptions rather than proving them for the max-linear regression model. Gebken's Goldstein-subdifferential analyses provide quantitative consequences of sharp (\(p=1\)) or higher-order growth combined with semismoothness-type properties, translating approximate criticality into distance-to-solution bounds; these results would become directly applicable once sharp growth is established for the regression objective. Trust-region and higher-order cutting-plane/bundle papers similarly show that sharp growth (or quadratic growth under nondegeneracy) implies strong local decrease properties and superlinear convergence of serious steps for finite max-type functions, again emphasizing the value of proving \(\mu>0\) for the target model.

Overall, none of the analyzed forward citations appears to settle the model-specific sharp growth verification for max-linear regression with \(\ell_1\) loss; the problem therefore remains open in the sense posed by the source paper. Promising directions suggested by the surrounding literature are to (i) exploit the piecewise-linear structure to reduce sharp growth to a polyhedral error bound (e.g., via metric subregularity of a KKT/normal-map system), and (ii) identify nondegeneracy conditions on the active max-indices and residual signs at \(\bar\beta\) that rule out flat directions (ensuring a positive linear growth modulus).

sources.title: Analyzing the speed of convergence in nonsmooth optimization via the Goldstein subdifferential with application to descent methods
sources.theorem_or_result: Main result (Theorem 1, as described in the provided excerpt): Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\) be locally Lipschitz and let \(x^*\) be a minimizer at which \(f\) satisfies a \(p\)-order growth condition (i.e., \(f(x)-f(x^*)\ge c\|x-x^*\|^p\) locally for some \(c>0\)) and a corresponding \(p\)-order semismoothness property at \(x^*\). If \(x_j\to x^*\), \(\varepsilon_j\to 0\), \(\delta_j\to 0\), and \(\min\|\partial_{\varepsilon_j} f(x_j)\|\le \delta_j\) (\((\varepsilon_j,\delta_j)\)-criticality), then there exists \(M>0\) such that \(\|x_j-x^*\|\le \max\{M\varepsilon_j^{1/p},\, M\delta_j^{1/(p-1)}\}\) (with the \(p=1\) “sharp minimum” case treated separately, where standard semismoothness suffices).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to verify a local sharp growth/error bound \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for a specific max-linear regression objective with absolute loss. Gebken (2025) does not analyze this statistical model or prove such an error bound for max-linear regression. Instead, it develops abstract relationships between \((\varepsilon,\delta)\)-criticality (via the Goldstein \(\varepsilon\)-subdifferential) and distance to a minimizer under assumed \(p\)-order growth (including the sharp \(p=1\) case) plus semismoothness-type conditions. Thus, the paper is relevant as a framework: if one can establish the desired sharp growth (\(p=1\)) for the max-linear absolute-loss objective (and verify semismoothness/piecewise differentiability, which is plausible for max/abs compositions), then the paper’s results can be used to quantify how quickly approximate Goldstein-critical points approach \(X^*\). But it does not provide the missing verification of \(\mu>0\) for that model.

sources.title: Analyzing the Speed of Convergence in Nonsmooth Optimization via the Goldstein subdifferential: B. Gebken
sources.bibtex:
@article{Gebken2025GoldsteinSpeedJOTA,
  title={Analyzing the Speed of Convergence in Nonsmooth Optimization via the Goldstein subdifferential},
  author={Gebken, B.},
  year={2025},
  doi={10.1007/s10957-025-02748-8},
  journal={Journal of Optimization Theory and Applications},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Main abstract result (Theorem 5.1): Let \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\) be locally Lipschitz and let \(x_j\to x^*\), \(\varepsilon_j\to0\), with \(\|g_{\varepsilon_j}(x_j)\|\le \delta_j\), where \(g_{\varepsilon}(x)\) is the minimum-norm element of the Goldstein \(\varepsilon\)-subdifferential \(\partial_{\varepsilon} f(x)\). Assume \(x^*\) is a local minimizer of order \(p\in\mathbb N\): \(f(x)\ge f(x^*)+\beta\|x-x^*\|^p\) locally, and a higher-order semismoothness-type condition (7): \[\liminf_{\xi\in\partial f(x^*+t d'),\ t\downarrow0,\ d'\to d}\ \frac{\langle \xi,d'\rangle}{t^{p-1}}\ge \beta\|d\|^p\quad\forall d\ne0.\] Then for \(p\ge2\) there exist \(M,N\) with \(\|x_j-x^*\|\le M\max(\varepsilon_j^{1/p},\delta_j^{1/(p-1)})\) for all \(j>N\); for \(p=1\) and \(\delta_j\to\bar\delta<\beta\), \(\|x_j-x^*\|\le M\varepsilon_j\) eventually. The paper also gives sufficient conditions for (7) for piecewise \(p\)-times \(C^p\) functions under a higher-order convexity condition (Lemma 4.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions ensuring a local sharp growth/error bound \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for the max-linear absolute-loss objective. This paper does not analyze that specific statistical model or objective, and it does not prove such an error bound for max-linear regression. However, it develops general theory linking (i) a linear growth/sharp minimum condition (the case \(p=1\) in Definition 3.1) and (ii) a semismoothness-type property to quantitative bounds on proximity to the minimizer from approximate criticality in the Goldstein \(\varepsilon\)-subdifferential. If one can establish the desired sharp growth for the max-linear absolute-loss objective (i.e., show it has a sharp minimum / order-1 growth around \(X^*\)), then the paper’s framework becomes applicable for analyzing convergence of Goldstein/gradient-sampling-type methods on that objective. The paper also provides verification tools for (7)/(8) for piecewise smooth max-type functions, which is structurally related to max-linear predictors, but it does not carry out the verification for the absolute-loss composition \(\sum_i |\bar y_i-\max_j\langle\beta_j,a_i\rangle|\).

sources.title: Enclosing minima in nonsmooth optimization via trust regions of higher-order cutting-plane models
sources.theorem_or_result:
For finite max-type functions \(f(x)=\max_{s\in S} f_s(x)\) (with \(S\) finite and \(f_s\in C^{q+1}\)), the paper proves that a trust-region decrease property (P) holds near a minimizer \(x^*\) under sharp growth (order \(p=1\)) or, under additional nondegeneracy assumptions, quadratic growth (order \(p=2\)). In particular:
- Theorem 4.1: If \(f\) has sharp growth around \(x^*\), i.e. \(f(x)\ge f(x^*)+\beta\|x-x^*\|\) locally, then (P) holds for \(p=1\).
- Theorem 4.2: If \(f\) has quadratic growth around \(x^*\) and satisfies Assumption (B2) (finite \(S\), full activity \(A(x^*)=S\) with strictly positive multiplier, affine independence of \(\nabla f_s(x^*)\)), then (P) holds for \(p=2\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions ensuring a local sharp growth/error bound \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for the max-linear regression objective with absolute loss. This paper does not analyze that statistical model or this specific objective. However, it develops general theory for finite max-type objectives: it shows that if one can establish sharp growth (order-1 growth) for a finite max-type function, then a related trust-region decrease property (P) follows (Thm. 4.1). Thus it provides a methodological bridge: proving sharp growth for the regression objective (or a reformulation as finite max-type) would imply (P), which their algorithm uses to generate shrinking trust regions containing the minimizer. The paper does not provide conditions on \(a_i,\bar y\) or on the max-linear absolute-loss structure that guarantee sharp growth, nor does it verify an explicit \(\mu\) for that problem.

sources.title: Superlinear convergence in nonsmooth optimization via higher-order cutting-plane models
sources.theorem_or_result: For a lower-C\(^2\) function \(f\) with a fixed global max-representation \(f(x)=\max_{s\in S} f_s(x)\) (Assumption (A1)) and a polynomial growth condition near a minimizer \(x^*\), the authors define the \(q\)-th order cutting-plane model \(T_{q,W}(z)=\max_{y\in W} T_q f_{s(y)}(z,y)\) and prove a Taylor-type uniform error bound on a trust region: for bounded \(V\subset U\) and \(\bar B_{\varepsilon}(x)\subset U\), there exists \(K\ge 0\) such that \(\max_{z\in \bar B_{\varepsilon}(x)} |f_W(z)-T_{q,W}(z)|\le K\varepsilon^{q+1}\) (Lemma 3.1). Using these higher-order max-models in a trust-region bundle framework yields local R-superlinear convergence of serious steps (and, for certain finite max-type functions, superlinear convergence of the overall sequence).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions ensuring a local sharp growth/error bound \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for the specific max-linear regression objective with absolute loss. This paper does not analyze that statistical objective or establish sharp growth for it. Instead, it develops higher-order cutting-plane (max-of-Taylor) models and proves superlinear convergence of a trust-region bundle method under an assumed growth condition (polynomial growth) for general lower-\u0002C\(^2\) max-type functions. Thus it is relevant mainly as methodology: if one could verify a sharp (linear) growth/error bound for the max-linear absolute-loss regression objective, then the papers framework suggests how to exploit such growth to obtain fast local convergence with appropriate bundle/trust-region methods.

sources.title: A Globalized Semismooth Newton Method for Prox-regular Optimization Problems
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops a globalized semismooth Newton framework for composite problems \(\min_x F(x)=f(x)+g(x)\) with \(f\in C^{2}\) having Lipschitz \(
abla f,
abla^2 f\) and \(g\) prox-regular and prox-bounded. A key technical result is: if \(h\) is prox-regular at \( ar x\), then for all sufficiently small \(\gamma>0\), the proximal mapping \(P_{\gamma h}\) is locally single-valued, locally Lipschitz, and monotone around \( ar x\) (Proposition 2.1). Using this, they define the proximal-gradient residual \(R_\gamma(x)=\gamma^{-1}(x-P_{\gamma g}(x-\gamma
abla f(x)))\) and propose Algorithm 1 (PGSSN), proving global convergence to an L-stationary point under a KL-exponent assumption (Theorem 4.1). Under an additional metric subregularity (error bound) assumption on \(R_*\) at a local minimizer \(x^*\), they prove local superlinear convergence of \(\mathrm{dist}(y_k,X^*)\) (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions ensuring a local sharp growth/error bound \(f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) for a specific max-linear regression objective with absolute loss. This paper does not analyze that statistical model or establish sharp growth for max-linear regression. It does, however, treat error bounds/metric subregularity as assumptions (Assumption 4(ii): \(\mathrm{dist}(x,X^*)\le \kappa\,\mathrm{dist}(0,R_*(x))\)) to obtain superlinear convergence of a Newton-type method. Thus it provides algorithmic tools and a convergence theory that would apply if one could verify an appropriate error bound/metric subregularity (or KL exponent \(1/2\)) for the max-linear absolute-loss objective, but it does not provide such verification.
